remediation_status.summary: Audit ran against `main` before the v0.7.0 cut. F1/F2/F3 are remediated on `dev` and ship in v0.7.0; F4/F5/F8 exploit path closed by #1159. Published as a historical posture snapshot, not open issues.

method: 6 parallel domain hunts + fresh-context adversarial verification of every surviving finding

findings.title: Unauthenticated agent-server reachable by peer agents -> cross-agent credential theft, RCE-as-peer, file tampering
findings.description: The per-agent FastAPI agent-server binds 0.0.0.0:8000 with no auth on any route (only permissive CORS). The backend sends no shared secret. All agents share a flat trinity-agent-network (plain bridge, ICC enabled, no internal:true). Any agent can therefore call a peer's GET /api/credentials/read?paths=.env to retrieve its plaintext credentials (external API keys, OAuth tokens, the victim's TRINITY_MCP_API_KEY), POST /api/chat to execute as the peer, and PUT /api/files to tamper. Bypasses the MCP-layer agent_permissions model entirely.
findings.exploit: Compromised/malicious agent A enumerates peers via Docker DNS or /16 subnet scan, then `curl http://agent-B:8000/api/credentials/read?paths=.env,.mcp.json` returns B's full secrets; B's MCP key enables further lateral movement as B.
findings.impact: Fleet-wide credential exfiltration and execution-as-peer from a single compromised agent; complete failure of agent-to-agent isolation; tenant-isolation breach in multi-user deployments.
findings.recommendation: Authenticate the agent-server (backend->agent shared secret or mTLS via router-level dependency) AND network-segment agents from each other (per-agent networks or L3/iptables blocking agent->agent:8000 while preserving agent->mcp-server:8080 / agent->otel:4317). Add a test asserting a peer cannot reach :8000.

remediation.note: AgentAuthMiddleware enforces per-agent X-Trinity-Agent-Token (HMAC) on every agent-server route except /health; permissive CORS removed. Cross-agent exploit closed.

findings.title: fastmcp (runtime dep) pulls HIGH hono/undici/form-data into the MCP server
findings.description: fastmcp@3.32.0 is a runtime/prod dependency; transitively pulls HIGH advisories hono (CORS credential reflection, JWT middleware scheme acceptance, static path traversal), undici (TLS cert-validation bypass, Set-Cookie injection, cache poisoning), form-data (CRLF). fastmcp is the MCP HTTP runtime so hono/undici are reachable. fixAvailable:true.
findings.exploit: Conditional on how fastmcp exercises hono/undici; advisories present on a live prod path.
findings.impact: Potential CORS/JWT/transport weaknesses in the MCP server HTTP surface.
findings.recommendation: npm audit fix / bump fastmcp in src/mcp-server/; re-run npm audit.

remediation.note: hono bump (#1255), undici bump (#1289), fastmcp now ^4.3.0.

findings.title: form-data (HIGH, CRLF) via axios ships in prod frontend bundle
findings.description: form-data@4.0.5 (HIGH CRLF) is transitive via axios@1.17.0, a production dependency in the nginx bundle. fixAvailable:true.
findings.exploit: Conditional on axios usage of vulnerable form-data path.
findings.impact: CRLF injection risk in outbound multipart requests from the shipped frontend.
findings.recommendation: npm audit fix / bump axios in src/frontend/.

findings.title: Agent-server EDIT_PROTECTED_PATHS diverges from backend deny-list (KEEP IN SYNC violated)
findings.description: Backend _FILE_WRITE_DENY_PATTERNS comment says KEEP IN SYNC with agent-server EDIT_PROTECTED_PATHS, but the agent-server list omits .ssh/*, .aws/*, .gcp/*, .claude/settings*.json. Since the agent-server is the real boundary for direct/peer callers (F1), writes to ~/.ssh/authorized_keys or ~/.aws/credentials are blocked at the backend proxy but ALLOWED at the agent-server.
findings.exploit: Peer agent (F1) PUTs .ssh/authorized_keys on a victim -> RCE-adjacent.
findings.impact: SSH/cloud-credential file overwrite on a peer agent.
findings.recommendation: Make EDIT_PROTECTED_PATHS a superset-or-equal of the backend list; ideally one shared glob validator across all agent-server file handlers.

remediation.note: No longer peer-reachable without the agent-auth token; validator hardening tracked as defense-in-depth follow-up.

findings.title: Sibling-prefix confinement bypass in 5 agent-server file handlers
findings.description: list_files/download_file/delete_file/preview_file/update_file gate with str(requested_path).startswith('/home/developer') (no trailing slash, base not resolved), so /home/developer-evil/x passes. ../ is separately blocked by .resolve(). create_folder (:449) was already fixed to is_relative_to() and its comment documents this exact bug class; the other five were not updated.
findings.exploit: Bounded today: writes to a new sibling EPERM-fail under root-owned /home + UID 1000; no sibling read targets are provisioned. Latent if a sibling-prefixed agent-readable mount is ever introduced.
findings.recommendation: Replace all five startswith checks with requested_path.is_relative_to(Path('/home/developer').resolve()).

remediation.note: No longer peer-reachable without the agent-auth token; validator hardening tracked as defense-in-depth follow-up.

findings.title: Secrets in pull_request-triggered jobs running PR-modifiable code; first-party actions on mutable tags
findings.description: E2E_ADMIN_PASSWORD/E2E_ANTHROPIC_API_KEY passed into pull_request-triggered jobs that execute start.sh + Dockerfiles (safe under default fork-secret policy; password masked). First-party actions/* pinned to mutable tags (rubric MEDIUM). Several workflows lack permissions: blocks. CODEOWNERS does not cover scripts/deploy/start.sh or docker/ that those workflows execute; single owner.
findings.exploit: Malicious same-repo branch or enabled fork-secrets setting could exfiltrate the secrets.
findings.impact: Potential CI secret exposure; tag-mutation supply-chain risk.
findings.recommendation: Gate secret steps on head.repo.full_name == github.repository; SHA-pin first-party actions; add permissions: blocks (esp deploy-dev.yml); extend CODEOWNERS to scripts/deploy + docker, add a 2nd owner.

findings.title: Three agent_config GET handlers leak any agent's config to any authenticated user
findings.description: GET capabilities/timeout/guardrails only Depends(get_current_user); no can_user_access_agent check (unlike resources/capacity/api-key GETs in the same file). Leaks capability flags, timeout, guardrail deny-lists, and agent-name existence (200 vs 404). Non-secret operational config -> LOW. Invariant #8 violation.
findings.exploit: Any authenticated user GETs /api/agents/<victim>/guardrails with their own JWT.
findings.impact: Cross-tenant disclosure of non-sensitive agent config.
findings.recommendation: Switch the three signatures to AuthorizedAgentByName (one line each).

findings.title: /api/credentials/update writes files with only lstrip('/') (no .. guard)
findings.description: update_credentials files loop does clean_path = file_path.lstrip('/') then writes home_dir/clean_path with no .. rejection or containment check, while the sibling read_credential_files (:202) blocks '..'. ../foo escapes lexically (bounded by root-owned FS perms).
findings.exploit: Peer/backend caller writes ../foo outside /home/developer (constrained by UID-1000 perms).
findings.recommendation: Copy the .. + is_relative_to guard from the read path.

remediation.note: No longer peer-reachable without the agent-auth token; validator hardening tracked as defense-in-depth follow-up.

findings.title: Slack email-verification code uses non-CSPRNG and has no attempt cap
findings.description: random.choices(string.digits, k=6) (Mersenne Twister) where db/email_auth.py:159 uses secrets.randbelow. Verify path has no attempt counter/lockout. MT-state recovery infeasible (codes emailed to victim); real weakness is unthrottled online brute-force of 10^6 within 10-min window.
findings.exploit: Brute-force the 6-digit code to bind an attacker Slack identity to a victim email and access email-gated agents.

findings.title: slack_service.download_file fetches event-supplied URL with bot token, no host allowlist
findings.description: GET url (from inbound event url_private_download) with Authorization: Bearer <bot_token> and follow_redirects=True, no host allowlist, unlike Telegram/WhatsApp adapters. Gated by HMAC-verified Slack events (attacker doesn't normally control url_private) -> defense-in-depth / token-leak-on-redirect.
findings.exploit: If url_private points off-Slack or 302-redirects off-domain, the bot token is sent to that host.
findings.impact: Bot-token leak on redirect; theoretical internal SSRF.

findings.title: OTP/login rate-limiters fail open when Redis unavailable
findings.description: check_otp_rate_limit / login limiter return True (allow) when Redis is None or raises. During a Redis outage, brute-force protection on the 6-digit OTP (10^6, 10-min window) evaporates. OTP itself is DB-backed, single-use, expiry-enforced.
findings.exploit: Attacker who can induce/await a Redis outage brute-forces the active OTP.
findings.recommendation: Fail closed for auth limiters, or back the OTP attempt counter in the DB row.

findings.title: SECRET_KEY placeholder default accepted with only a warning
findings.description: If SECRET_KEY equals the literal placeholder, config prints CRITICAL but still uses it -> world-known JWT signing key (forgeable tokens). Unset key safely auto-generates random.
findings.exploit: Operator who sets the placeholder ships a forgeable-JWT instance.
findings.recommendation: raise / refuse to boot on the known placeholder (match Redis-password discipline).

findings.title: Telegram webhook secret-token compared with != (non-constant-time)
findings.description: 256-bit secret token compared with != rather than hmac.compare_digest. Timing attack infeasible on a 256-bit secret + second URL-secret factor. Hygiene only.
findings.recommendation: Use hmac.compare_digest to match the Slack transport.

supply_chain_summary.python_pins: pinned inline in docker/backend/Dockerfile; no reachable HIGH; python-jose CVEs neutralized by HS256-only + explicit algorithm allowlist + no JWE

filter_stats.verifier_passes: fresh-context refutation on F1, F4, F5, F7, F8, F9, F10 + linchpin network-reachability proof

secrets_archaeology.note: All prefix hits are documented placeholders or test fixtures; no incident-response playbook required.

trend.note: F1 never previously flagged; #589 isolation work scoped to agents<->Redis only, not agent<->agent. Deps F2/F3 are new advisories. Strong historical remediation track record (589 OBS-1/2/3 all resolved in-cycle).
